subject: social-medicine
title: Social Medicine & Public Health — High-Yield Notes
description: Condensed revision notes across 21 topics — health & prevention, epidemiology & screening, communicable/non-communicable disease, global health, surveillance, and study methods.

sections.title: Core definitions
sections.html:
Health (WHO 1946/48)

"A state of complete physical, mental and social well-being and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity." A holistic definition → more than diagnosis & therapy is needed.

Public health vs Population health

Population health describes the condition — the health of a population as measured by indicators. Public health is the activities/policies/programs society undertakes to assure the conditions in which people can be healthy.

Health promotion (Ottawa Charter, 1st Intl Conference, Ottawa 1986)

Enables people to increase control over their own health. Covers a wide range of social & environmental interventions addressing the root causes of ill health, not just treatment/cure. 3 pillars: good governance for healthhealth literacyhealthy cities

Health education

An indispensable tool of health promotion: consciously constructed learning opportunities to improve health literacy, knowledge & life skills. Methods: mass campaigns, group, individual.

Global health

Health problems that transcend national boundaries; best addressed by cooperative action.

Community (definition)

May refer to a geographical area OR a group of people with common characteristics — shared interest (religion, sport, art) or a particular disease.

Clinical medicine vs Public health / Social medicine

ClinicalPublic health – Social medicine
Focus on the individualFocus on the population
Disease diagnosis & treatmentHealth, prevention, promotion for patient + community
Emphasis on medical careSpectrum: environment + behaviour/lifestyle + medical care

"Public Health – Social Medicine" is the Greek medical specialty of the field of public health.

sections.title: Determinants of health
sections.html:
The river metaphor

Moving from downstream medicine (treating illness) to upstream health (addressing root causes).

Contribution of drivers to health status

Only ~15–25% healthcare. The physical environment, social determinants & behavioural factors drive ~80% of outcomes.

DomainApprox contribution
Health care10–25%
Socioeconomic / social circumstances~45–50%
Health behaviours~40%
Environmental exposure~10%
Genetics~15%

Social Determinants of Health (SDOH — WHO)

The non-medical conditions in which people are born, grow, work, live and age, and the wider forces shaping daily life (economic policy, social norms, political systems).

Equality vs Equity

Equality = sameness (equal access for all). Equity = fairness (adjusting resources for disadvantaged groups to create a level playing field).

Inequalities = all measurable differences in health. Inequities = differences that are unfair, avoidable & systematic (socially modifiable).

Social gradient

Graded relationship — health outcomes progressively improve with increasing social position.

Syndemic

Synergistic epidemic: clustering of ≥2 diseases in a population, worsened by social/economic/environmental factors → greater burden than the sum of individual epidemics (e.g. SAVA: Substance abuse–Violence–AIDS).

Behavioural determinants — proximal vs distal

Proximal = immediate individual behaviours (smoking, diet). Distal = underlying structural drivers (SES, environment, food deserts).

COM-B model

A behaviour needs Capability + Opportunity + Motivation. Basis of the Behaviour Change Wheel.

sections.title: Levels of prevention
sections.html:
LevelWhen / questionExamples
Primordial"Change the game" — stop risks ever developingUrban planning, poverty reduction, banning high-risk products
PrimaryPre-event — prevent onset in healthy peopleVaccination, no smoking, seatbelt laws, safe food, HPV vaccine
SecondaryEarly detection while asymptomaticScreening (mammogram, Pap, PSA), BP checks
TertiaryMinimise disability once diagnosedRehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease management
QuaternaryProtect from over-medicalisation / unnecessary careAvoiding overdiagnosis & overtreatment
Exam traps: Vaccination = PRIMARY prevention. Screening = SECONDARY. HPV vaccine for cervical cancer = PRIMARY (it's a vaccine, even though the target is a cancer). Preventive mastectomy = PRIMARY (prophylactic removal) — so it is NOT part of secondary prevention of breast cancer.

Lifestyle change (stop smoking, weight, diet, exercise) in a woman with a history of gestational diabetes = primary prevention.

sections.title: Epidemiology basics & rates
sections.html:
Epidemiology (Last)

Study of the distribution (frequency + pattern) and determinants of health-related states in specified populations, and the application to control health problems. "The basic science of public health." 5 W's: what, who, where, when, why/how.

Morbidity measures

Prevalence = all cases (new + old) ÷ total population at a time point. Useful for chronic disease.

Incidence = new cases ÷ population at risk over a period. Useful for acute disease. Only cohort (prospective) studies can calculate incidence.

Mortality measures

Mortality (cause-specific) = deaths from a cause ÷ total population (e.g. per 100,000/yr). Case fatality = deaths ÷ number of cases of that disease.

DALYs

Disability-Adjusted Life Years = years of life lost (premature death) + years lived with disability. 1 DALY = 1 lost year of healthy life.

sections.title: Screening & diagnostic test validity
sections.html:
Screening (secondary prevention)

Applying tests to detect disease/precursors in the asymptomatic phase. Does not diagnose — determines need for further investigation. Wilson & Jungner principles govern which conditions to screen.

2×2 table

Disease +Disease −
Test +a (true +)b (false +)
Test −c (false −)d (true −)

The four metrics

  • Sensitivity = a/(a+c) → % of diseased correctly called positive. High Se needed for a screening test (don't miss cases).
  • Specificity = d/(b+d) → % of healthy correctly called negative. High Sp needed for a confirmatory test.
  • PPV = a/(a+b) → given a positive result, probability disease is truly present. ↑ with ↑ prevalence.
  • NPV = d/(c+d) → given a negative result, probability disease is truly absent. ↑ with ↓ prevalence.
"70% sensitivity" means: the test correctly identifies 70% of people who truly have the disease; the other 30% are missed (false negatives).

Cut-off trade-off

Raising the cut-off ↓ sensitivity, ↑ specificity (and vice-versa). Se & Sp are intrinsic to the test; PPV & NPV depend on prevalence.

PSA example (overdiagnosis)

PSA is highly sensitive (~93%) but poorly specific (~20%). Combined with slow-growing tumours → low specificity leads to overdiagnosis and overtreatment; most men die with not of prostate cancer.

Errors

Lab errors: pre-analytical ~60%, post-analytical ~30%, analytical ~10%. Gold/reference standard = best available comparator, not perfect.

sections.title: Communicable diseases
sections.html:
Epidemiological triad & chain of infection

Triad: Agent – Host – Environment. Chain: agent → reservoir → exit portal → mode of transmission → entry portal → susceptible host.

Agent properties

Infectivity (ID50), Pathogenicity (attack rate), Virulence (case-fatality rate).

Reservoirs — 3 types

Human, Animal, Environmental. Zoonoses = animal→human; account for ~60% of infectious diseases & 75% of emerging ones.

Transmission

Direct (contact, droplet <1.5 m, transplacental, bite) vs Indirect (vector, vehicle, airborne). Rubella (Ερυθρά) → direct droplet contact.

Patterns in time/space

Sporadicoccasional, unpredictable
Endemicpredictable regularity (R=1)
Epidemic / Outbreakcases above baseline (outbreak = smaller area)
Pandemicacross countries/continents

R0 and R

R0 = avg secondary infections in a fully susceptible population. R = actual number accounting for immunity. R>1 grows, R=1 endemic, R<1 declines.

Herd (community) immunity

Population-level resistance when enough are immune to break transmission (R<1). Full 100% coverage not required; threshold depends on the agent. Active immunity = host makes antibodies after infection/vaccination; passive = ready-made antibodies given.

Elimination vs Eradication

Elimination = incidence to zero in a defined area (measles, polio). Eradication = global incidence to zero (smallpox). Extinction = agent gone even from labs (none).

sections.title: NCDs & cancer
sections.html:
NCDs

Chronic, long-duration, slow progress. Top 4 killers: cardiovascular (17.9M), cancers (9.0M), respiratory (3.9M), diabetes (1.6M). Four shared behaviours: tobacco, physical inactivity, unhealthy diet, harmful alcohol.

Tumours

Benign = stays in primary site. Malignant = invades & metastasises (via blood/lymph; commonly liver, lung, brain, bone). Carcinoma (epithelial) = 80–90% of cancers.

3 classes of carcinogens

Physical / natural — UV, ionizing radiation, asbestosChemical — tobacco, arsenic, aflatoxinBiological — HPV, HBV/HCV, EBV, H. pylori, Schistosoma

Nitrosamine from tobacco combustion → carcinogenic. Nitrogen oxides in cigarette gases → damage the respiratory system. Smoking → coronary heart disease / angina.

Risk factors

Modifiable (tobacco, alcohol, obesity, diet, inactivity, infections) vs non-modifiable (age, genetics). Obesity is a risk factor in the adult Greek population; exercise & high fruit/veg are protective.

Diet & cancer

Increased fruit consumption → reduced appearance of certain cancer types (not "all cancers"). Mediterranean diet (Seven Countries Study, Ancel Keys) → lower CVD; UNESCO intangible heritage.

Leading cancer death

Lung (trachea/bronchus/lung) is the leading cause of cancer death; risen from 1.2M (2000) to 1.9M (2021). Pancreatic ~5% 5-yr survival; colon early detection ~90% 5-yr.

sections.title: Cardiovascular disease prevention
sections.html:
Primary vs Secondary (CVD)

Primary = measures in patients without diagnosed CVD. Secondary = in patients with a diagnosis.

Modifiable vs non-modifiable risk factors

ModifiableNon-modifiable
Smoking, dyslipidemia, HTN, diabetes, obesity, inactivityAge, sex/gender, family history of early CVD, low SES
Exam favourite: among {dyslipidemia, gender, family history, low BMI} the modifiable one is dyslipidemia. Low BMI is not a CHD risk factor; gender & family history are non-modifiable.

SCORE risk model

Estimates 10-yr CVD risk from: age, sex, smoking, systolic BP (arterial hypertension), cholesterol. → Among {thyroid, obesity, hypertension, diabetes}, SCORE includes hypertension.

Smoking & CVD

Doubles CHD incidence, +50% CHD mortality (thrombosis + vasoconstriction). Quitting halves risk; approaches baseline in 5–15 yrs. Max blood nicotine concentration reached within ~10 minutes. Cigarette smoke = mainstream + sidestream.

sections.title: Injuries & the burden of injury
sections.html:
Injury definition

A bodily lesion from acute overexposure to energy (mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical, radiant) beyond the body's tolerance. Intentional or unintentional.

"Accidents are not just accidents"

Old view: accidents = random, inevitable, fate. Modern view: injuries are predictable and preventable (specific energy transfers that can be blocked). Rejecting "accident" is the key conceptual shift.

Haddon matrix

12 cells = 3 phases (pre-event, event, post-event) × 4 factors (host, agent/energy, physical env, socio-economic env).

The "3 Es"

EngineeringEducationEnforcement

Epidemiology

  • ~4.3–4.4M deaths/yr (~8% global), ~10% of years lived with disability.
  • Ages 5–29: 3 of the top 5 causes of death are injury-related (road traffic, homicide, suicide). Road traffic injuries = leading cause of death for ages 5–29.
  • Ages 1–44 (US): injuries kill more than any other cause.
  • Most-affected age group by injury DALYs (2023): 15–49 yrs (~55%).
  • Twice as many males as females die from injuries.
  • Falls: leading non-fatal injury/DALYs in elderly (70+); key RF = low bone mineral density.
Road traffic accidents show: local clustering & temporal periodicity; ↑ in summer months; higher on provincial roads; those most prone = men. In the US, ~1/5 of fatal crashes involve teenagers/adolescents.

sections.title: Public health surveillance
sections.html:
Definition (CDC)

The ongoing systematic collection, analysis and interpretation of data, closely integrated with timely dissemination to those responsible for preventing & controlling disease and injury.

The order (exam recall): Collection → Analysis → Interpretation → Dissemination → (Link to action). Surveillance seeks action; research seeks generalizable knowledge.

Analysis dimensions

By Time (epidemic curves), Place (spot/GIS maps), Person (age, sex, occupation).

System types

TypeKey point
PassiveRoutine notifications from physicians; cheap, wide, but incomplete/slow
ActiveStaff actively seek data (sentinel networks); most accurate/timely but expensive
SyndromicBased on symptoms not lab confirmation; timely (bioterrorism) but low specificity
Behavioural risk factor (BRFSS)Repeated surveys of behaviours; predicts outcomes early

Attributes

Usefulness, data quality, timeliness, flexibility, simplicity, stability, sensitivity, PPV, representativeness, acceptability. Trade-off: the more sensitive the system, the lower its PPV.

Sources

EU: ECDC-TESSy, EuroMOMO (excess mortality), EARS-Net (AMR). Greece: EODY (notifications ΣΥΔΝ + sentinel), ELSTAT (vital stats), IDIKA (e-prescription).

sections.title: Outbreaks, PHEIC, CERC & infodemic
sections.html:
Outbreak investigation — the steps

Classic sequence: verify the diagnosis & confirm the outbreak exists → establish/verify a case definition & count cases → describe by time/place/person → generate & test hypotheses → implement control measures → communicate. The first step is to confirm the outbreak & verify the diagnosis; an early essential is defining a case definition.

Case definition

A standard set of criteria (clinical + time + place + person) used to decide whether a person counts as a case — makes counting consistent.

PHEIC & IHR (2005)

PHEIC = extraordinary event that is a public health risk to other states through international spread & may need a coordinated response. Declared by the WHO Director-General, advised by the Emergency Committee (IHR Art. 48). Notify WHO within 24 h. Temporary Recommendations reviewed every 3 months. COVID-19 PHEIC: declared 30 Jan 2020, pandemic 11 Mar 2020, ended 5 May 2023.

Infodemic vs mis/disinformation

Infodemic = too much info (incl. false) during an outbreak. Misinformation = false, no intent to harm. Disinformation = knowingly false, intent to deceive/harm. Difference = intent & knowledge of the sender.

CERC — 6 principles (CDC)

Be FirstBe RightBe CredibleExpress EmpathyPromote ActionShow Respect — "Prebunk before you debunk."

sections.title: Emergency preparedness & disaster management
sections.html:
Terms

Emergency = serious unexpected event needing immediate action. Disaster = disruption exceeding a community's own coping capacity. Crisis = broader threat to normal functioning. Key issue = whether the event overwhelms coping capacity.

Risk equation components

Hazard × Exposure × Vulnerability ÷ Capacity = Risk.

Disaster management cycle (4 phases)

Prevention/Mitigation → Preparedness → Response → Recovery. Preparedness makes effective response possible.

Key approaches

Whole-health · All-hazards · Whole-of-government / whole-of-society · One Health.

Floods = most common natural disaster in the WHO European Region.

sections.title: Quality of care & patient safety
sections.html:
The 7 dimensions of quality (IOM) — "STEEEPI"

S — SafeT — TimelyE — EfficientE — EffectiveE — EquitableP — People-centredI — Integrated

Quality of care = the degree to which health services increase the likelihood of desired health outcomes.

Founders

Ernest Codman — father of QI (end-result note cards). Avedis Donabedian — Structure–Process–Outcome model.

Understanding the system — QI tools

Combine methods of inquiry: run charts, surveys, observation, fishbone diagram, brainstorming. Ideal understanding comes from combining methods. Brainstorming improves team dynamics.

Fishbone (Ishikawa / cause-and-effect) diagram: the head holds the problem / effect being analysed; the "bones" are categories of contributing causes. It is a tool for understanding the system. (Not for it: it IS used — the "NOT used" distractor in exams was a run chart/observation vs a genuine non-tool.)

Culture

Move from blame culture → focus on the system/methods. Swiss cheese model (Reason): harm occurs when holes in successive defences line up → improve systems, not just "be careful".

Key facts

1 in 10 hospitalised patients harmed (high-income); up to 4 in 10 harmed in primary/outpatient care; ~50% of adverse events preventable. Most detrimental errors: diagnosis, prescription, medication use.

sections.title: Health promotion & health education planning
sections.html:
Planning steps (Health Education plan)

Identify the target group → needs assessment → determine objectives → determine activities → determine resources → determine evaluation measures.

Objectives are grouped by

Expected change in knowledge/awareness, attitudes/values, and behaviour. They presuppose decision-making + behaviour change.

Activities

Informational (lectures, mass media, posters → awareness/knowledge) vs Educational/skills (role-play, contracting → attitudes & behaviour).

Evaluation

Impact = immediate effects (awareness, knowledge, behaviour). Outcome = end results (morbidity, mortality). Mixed methods: qualitative + quantitative.

How to stop smoking in a community works at population level: smoke-free public-place laws (protect non-smokers, help quitting, shift the norm), tobacco taxation, graphic pack warnings, mass-media campaigns, cessation support — a mix of primordial/primary prevention + the Behaviour Change Wheel (education, persuasion, restriction, environmental restructuring).

sections.title: Food safety & public health
sections.html:
Definitions

Food safety = assurance food will not cause adverse health effects when prepared/eaten as intended (FAO/WHO Codex). Foodborne diseases = caused by food contamination at any point in the chain. Bacteria are the most common food-safety hazard.

Global burden (WHO FERG)

31 hazards → 32 diseases: 11 diarrheal, 7 invasive infectious, 10 helminths, 3 chemicals.

Governance

"Farm to Fork" chain. Codex Alimentarius (food safety), IPPC (plant health), WOAH (animal health) under the WTO SPS Agreement. EU Food Law = Reg 178/2002 (risk analysis, precaution, traceability, transparency). EFSA + ECDC publish the One Health Zoonoses Report.

Most common foodborne / reported zoonosis in the EU: Campylobacter (campylobacteriosis) — per the annual EFSA/ECDC One Health Zoonoses Report.

sections.title: WHO & global health governance
sections.html:
WHO structure

UN specialized agency (constitution in force 7 Apr 1948, World Health Day). 194 member states. World Health Assembly (WHA) = supreme decision-making body. Executive Board (34 members). Director-General. 6 regional offices: AFRO, AMRO, EMRO, EURO, SEARO, WPRO.

Selected achievements

Smallpox eradicated (1979/80), Global Polio Eradication Initiative (1988), ICD-11 (adopted 2019, in force 1 Jan 2022).

Hierarchy: normative vs regulatory

LevelRole / example
GlobalWHO — sets international standards (normative)
RegionalWHO Europe, EU DG SANTE, ECDC, EMA
NationalMinistries of Health — laws & budgets
LocalMunicipal boards / primary care — implementation

Greece: Ministry of Health → EODY (surveillance/prevention), EOPYY (purchaser), 7 YPEs (regional), ESY (national health system).

sections.title: One Health & the SDGs
sections.html:
One Health

Integrated, multisectoral approach recognising the interdependence of human, animal, plant & ecosystem health. Tackles zoonoses, AMR, food safety at the human–animal–environment interface. Term first used by James Steele (1947).

Quadripartite: WHO + FAO + WOAH + UNEP. EU cross-agency One Health task force (EEA, ECDC, ECHA, EFSA, EMA).

AMR (antimicrobial resistance) arises from the misuse & overuse of antimicrobials — a flagship One Health issue; resistant germs spread through people, food, healthcare & the environment.

Sustainable Development & SDGs

Meeting present needs without compromising future generations — balancing economic growth, social inclusion, environmental protection. The 2030 Agenda (adopted by all 193 UN states, 2015) contains 17 SDGs & 169 targets across 5 P's: People, Planet, Prosperity, Peace, Partnership. SDG 3 = Good Health & Well-being.

sections.title: Demography & maternal-child health
sections.html:
Demographic transition — second phase

Characterised by high fertility with declining mortality (population grows). Progression: high birth+death → high birth, falling death → falling birth & death → low birth & death.

Population pyramid — mature population

Characterised by declining mortality followed gradually by a sharp fall in fertility (narrowing base, ageing structure).

Vital terms

In medical demography, "births" = live births (γεννήσεις ζώντων βρεφών). A country's birth rate is influenced by population composition (incl. proportion of women of reproductive age).

Maternal & child health

  • Maternal health = health of women during pregnancy, childbirth and the postnatal (postpartum) period.
  • Neonatal mortality = deaths in the first 28 days of life (early neonatal <7 days; late 7–<28). Infant mortality = deaths in the first year.

Life expectancy

Life expectancy at birth = avg lifespan of a newborn given current mortality; generally higher in women. Healthy life expectancy (HALE) = years lived in good health — answers "does improving health come with longevity?"

Greece — family planning

First institutionalised in 1980, Law 1036.

sections.title: Healthy ageing
sections.html:
Healthy ageing (WHO)

The process of developing and maintaining functional ability that enables wellbeing in older age. A universal right — not the absence of disease. WHO's focus 2015–2030 (superseded the Active Ageing framework of 2002).

Functional ability = Intrinsic capacity × Environment

Intrinsic capacity = all mental & physical capacities (walking, thinking, seeing, hearing, memory). Environment = home, community, society (built environment, relationships, policies) — can be barrier or facilitator.

Pillars: meet basic needs · learn & grow · be mobile · build relationships · contribute to society. Driven by the demographic transition to low fertility + increased longevity.

sections.title: Rogers' diffusion of innovation
sections.html:
Psychology of change — Rogers' diffusion of innovation curve

When a new (clearly improved) idea is introduced, people adopt it at different rates along a bell curve:

Group≈ %
Innovators2.5%
Early adopters13.5%
Early majority34%
Late majority34%
Laggards16%
Exam recall: a small percentage (~16% = laggards) will stubbornly resist change and may never adopt it. Improvement does not require the entire population to adopt; innovative ideas do meet resistance. So "adoption rarely meets obstacles" and "everyone reacts the same" are FALSE.

sections.title: Study designs & methods
sections.html:
Measures of association

Cohort → relative risk & incidence. Case-control → odds ratio. Prospective cohort's advantage: can calculate incidence.

Nested case-control

Designed within a cohort study.

Meta-analysis

Quantitative synthesis of results from individual studies → a weighted average where larger studies get more weight. Umbrella reviews (overviews of systematic reviews): synthesis unit = systematic reviews.

Bradford-Hill criteria

9 criteria for causation, including strength, temporality, specificity, plausibility, consistency, biological gradient, coherence, experiment, analogy.

Confounding

A confounder must be associated with the exposure, be an independent risk factor for the outcome, and not lie on the causal pathway (not an intermediate).
